title: Reading a financial statement without getting lost
subtitle: Three documents tell you almost everything about a business. Knowing what to look for — and what to ignore — is the practical skill.

body.content: Every public company publishes three financial statements: the income statement, the balance sheet, and the cash flow statement. Together, they provide a complete picture of a company's financial position and performance. Most investors look at one, ignore the second, and have never opened the third. This is a significant gap.

body.label: The three statements
body.content: Income statement: what the company earned and spent over a period. Balance sheet: what the company owns and owes at a point in time. Cash flow statement: how cash actually moved in and out — regardless of accounting recognition. Each tells a different part of the story. None is complete on its own.
What to look for in each

items: Income statement: revenue growth rate, gross margin trend, operating leverage (does profit grow faster than revenue?), and whether earnings per share growth is organic or driven by buybacks.

Balance sheet: debt-to-equity ratio, current ratio (can it meet short-term obligations?), goodwill as a fraction of total assets (large goodwill can signal acquisition risk), and whether book value is growing.

Cash flow statement: free cash flow (operating cash flow minus capital expenditure), cash conversion (does accounting income translate to actual cash?), and whether the company is self-financing or dependent on external capital.

body.content: The most important rule: when accounting earnings and cash flow diverge significantly over multiple periods, trust the cash flow statement. Earnings can be managed through accounting choices. Cash is harder to manufacture.

Financial statements are not reports to endure — they are a conversation with the business. The questions you bring to them determine how much they tell you. Start with the cash flow statement. Everything else follows.
